***[2025–26 European Rugby Champions Cup](/wiki/2025%E2%80%9326_European_Rugby_Champions_Cup "2025–26 European Rugby Champions Cup")*** | |

(known as the **Investec Champions Cup** for sponsorship reasons) is an annual [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") tournament organised by [European Professional Club Rugby](/wiki/European_Professional_Club_Rugby "European Professional Club Rugby") (EPCR). It is the top-tier competition for clubs who compete in a predominantly European league. Clubs qualify for the Champions Cup via their final positions in their respective national/regional leagues ([English Premiership](/wiki/Premiership_Rugby "Premiership Rugby"), French [Top 14](/wiki/Top_14 "Top 14"), and [United Rugby Championship](/wiki/United_Rugby_Championship "United Rugby Championship")) or via winning the second-tier [Challenge Cup](/wiki/European_Rugby_Challenge_Cup "European Rugby Challenge Cup"); those that do not qualify are instead eligible to compete in the second-tier Challenge Cup.

and was run by [European Rugby Cup](/wiki/European_Rugby_Cup "European Rugby Cup"). Following [disagreements](#Disagreements_over_structure_&_governance) between its shareholders over the structure and governance of the competition, it was taken over by EPCR and its name was changed to the European Rugby Champions Cup, without title sponsorship. Heineken returned as sponsor for the 2018–19 season, resulting in the competition being known as the Heineken Champions Cup. Although they are technically two separate competitions, run by different organisations, the European Rugby Cup and the European Rugby Champions Cup are recognised as one title chain of elite club championships in Europe, with teams that have won multiple titles ranked, for example, by the aggregate of their wins in both versions.

(Redirected from [Heineken Cup](/w/index.php?title=Heineken_Cup&redirect=no "Heineken Cup"))

Annual rugby union tournament

European Rugby Champions Cup

The **European Rugby Champions Cup** (known as the **Investec Champions Cup** for sponsorship reasons) is an annual [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") tournament organised by [European Professional Club Rugby](/wiki/European_Professional_Club_Rugby "European Professional Club Rugby") (EPCR). It is the top-tier competition for clubs who compete in a predominantly European league. Clubs qualify for the Champions Cup via their final positions in their respective national/regional leagues ([English Premiership](/wiki/Premiership_Rugby "Premiership Rugby"), French [Top 14](/wiki/Top_14 "Top 14"), and [United Rugby Championship](/wiki/United_Rugby_Championship "United Rugby Championship")) or via winning the second-tier [Challenge Cup](/wiki/European_Rugby_Challenge_Cup "European Rugby Challenge Cup"); those that do not qualify are instead eligible to compete in the second-tier Challenge Cup.

Between 1995 and 2014, the equivalent competition was known as the **Heineken Cup** and was run by [European Rugby Cup](/wiki/European_Rugby_Cup "European Rugby Cup"). Following [disagreements](#Disagreements_over_structure_&_governance) between its shareholders over the structure and governance of the competition, it was taken over by EPCR and its name was changed to the European Rugby Champions Cup, without title sponsorship. Heineken returned as sponsor for the 2018–19 season, resulting in the competition being known as the Heineken Champions Cup. Although they are technically two separate competitions, run by different organisations, the European Rugby Cup and the European Rugby Champions Cup are recognised as one title chain of elite club championships in Europe, with teams that have won multiple titles ranked, for example, by the aggregate of their wins in both versions.

French clubs have the highest number of victories (13 wins), followed by England (10 wins) and Ireland (7 wins). England has the largest number of winning teams, with six clubs having won the title. The competition has been won by thirteen different clubs, eight of which have won it more than once, and five successfully defended their title including a unique three-in-a-row made by [Toulon](/wiki/RC_Toulonnais "RC Toulonnais") between [2012–13](/wiki/2012%E2%80%9313_Heineken_Cup "2012–13 Heineken Cup") and [2014–15](/wiki/2014%E2%80%9315_European_Rugby_Champions_Cup "2014–15 European Rugby Champions Cup"). [Toulouse](/wiki/Stade_Toulousain "Stade Toulousain") is the most successful club in the tournament's history, having won it 6 times, including the first season of the tournament in the [1995–96](/wiki/1995%E2%80%9396_Heineken_Cup "1995–96 Heineken Cup") season. [Bordeaux](/wiki/Union_Bordeaux_B%C3%A8gles "Union Bordeaux Bègles") are the current European champions, having beaten [Northampton Saints](/wiki/Northampton_Saints "Northampton Saints") 28–20 in the [2025 final](/wiki/2025_European_Rugby_Champions_Cup_final "2025 European Rugby Champions Cup final") in [Cardiff](/wiki/Cardiff "Cardiff"), [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales").
[Toulouse](/wiki/Stade_Toulousain "Stade Toulousain") have completed "the Double" (Heineken Cup/European Rugby Champions Cup-National Championship) 3 times (1995–96, 2020–21 and 2023–24), a record in Europe.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Rugby_Champions_Cup&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Heineken Cup

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Rugby_Champions_Cup&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Heineken Cup")]

#### 1995–1999

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Rugby_Champions_Cup&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1995–1999")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/e/e3/Heineken_cup.png)](/wiki/File:Heineken_cup.png)

The Heineken Cup logo used until 2013

The Heineken Cup was launched in the summer of 1995 on the initiative of the then Five Nations Committee to provide a new level of professional cross border competition.[[1]](#cite_note-historymain-1) Twelve sides representing Ireland, Wales, Italy, Romania and France competed in four pools of three with the group winners going directly into the semi-finals.[[2]](#cite_note-historyone-2) English and Scottish teams did not take part in the inaugural competition.[[3]](#cite_note-hcup-3) From an inauspicious beginning in Romania, where [Toulouse](/wiki/Stade_Toulousain "Stade Toulousain") defeated [Farul Constanţa](/wiki/Farul_Constan%C5%A3a_(rugby) "Farul Constanţa (rugby)") 54–10 in front of a small crowd, the competition gathered momentum and crowds grew. Toulouse went on to become the first European cup winners, eventually beating [Cardiff](/wiki/Cardiff_RFC "Cardiff RFC") in extra time in front of a crowd of 21,800 at Cardiff Arms Park.[[2]](#cite_note-historyone-2)

Clubs from England and Scotland joined the competition in 1996–97.[[4]](#cite_note-historyarticle-4) European rugby was further expanded with the advent of the [European Challenge Cup](/wiki/European_Challenge_Cup "European Challenge Cup") for teams that did not qualify for the Heineken Cup. The Heineken Cup now had 20 teams divided into four pools of five.[[5]](#cite_note-historytwo-5) Only Leicester and Brive reached the knock-out stages with 100 per cent records and ultimately made it to the final, Cardiff and Toulouse falling in the semi-finals. After 46 matches, [Brive](/wiki/CA_Brive "CA Brive") beat Leicester 28–9 in front of a crowd of 41,664 at [Cardiff Arms Park](/wiki/Cardiff_Arms_Park "Cardiff Arms Park"), the match watched by an estimated television audience of 35 million in 86 countries.[[5]](#cite_note-historytwo-5)

The season 1997–98 saw the introduction of a home and away format in the pool games.[[6]](#cite_note-historythree-6) The five pools of four teams, which guaranteed each team a minimum of six games, and the three quarter-final play-off matches all added up to a 70-match tournament. Brive reached the final again but were beaten late in the game by [Bath](/wiki/Bath_Rugby "Bath Rugby") with a penalty kick. Ironically, English clubs had decided to withdraw from the competition in a dispute over the way it was run.[[3]](#cite_note-hcup-3)

Without English clubs, the 1998–99 tournament revolved around France, Italy and the Celtic nations. Sixteen teams took part in four pools of four. French clubs filled the top positions in three of the groups and for the fourth consecutive year a French club, in the shape of [Colomiers](/wiki/US_Colomiers "US Colomiers") from the Toulouse suburbs, reached the final. Despite this it was to be [Ulster](/wiki/Ulster_Rugby "Ulster Rugby")'s year as they beat Toulouse (twice) and reigning French champions [Stade Français](/wiki/Stade_Fran%C3%A7ais "Stade Français") on their way to the final at [Lansdowne Road](/wiki/Lansdowne_Road "Lansdowne Road"), Dublin. Ulster then carried home the trophy after a 21–6 win over Colomiers in front of a capacity 49,000 crowd.[[6]](#cite_note-historythree-6)

#### 1999–2004

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Rugby_Champions_Cup&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1999–2004")]

English clubs returned in 1999–00. The pool stages were spread over three months to allow the competition to develop alongside the nations' own domestic competitions, and the knockout stages were scheduled to take the tournament into the early spring. For the first time clubs from four nations – England, Ireland, France and Wales – made it through to the semi-finals. Munster's defeat of Toulouse in Bordeaux ended France's record of having contested every final and [Northampton Saints](/wiki/Northampton_Saints "Northampton Saints")' victory over [Llanelli](/wiki/Llanelli_RFC "Llanelli RFC") made them the third English club to make it to the final. The competition was decided with a final between [Munster](/wiki/Munster_Rugby "Munster Rugby") and Northampton, with Northampton coming out on top by a single point to claim their first major honour.[[4]](#cite_note-historyarticle-4)

England supplied two of the 2000–01 semi-finalists – [Leicester Tigers](/wiki/Leicester_Tigers "Leicester Tigers") and [Gloucester](/wiki/Gloucester_RFC "Gloucester RFC") – with Munster and French champions Stade Français also reaching the last four. Both semi-finals were close, Munster going down by a point 16–15 to Stade Français in [Lille](/wiki/Lille "Lille") and the Tigers beating Gloucester 19–15 at [Vicarage Road](/wiki/Vicarage_Road "Vicarage Road"), Watford. The final, at [Parc des Princes](/wiki/Parc_des_Princes "Parc des Princes"), Paris, attracted a crowd of 44,000 and the result was in the balance right up until the final whistle, but Leicester walked off 34–30 winners.

Munster reached the 2001–02 final with quarter-final and semi-final victories on French soil against Stade Français and [Castres](/wiki/Castres_Olympique "Castres Olympique"). Leicester pipped Llanelli in the last four, after the Scarlets had halted Leicester's 11-match Heineken Cup winning streak in the pool stages. A record crowd saw Leicester become the first side to successfully defend their title.[[1]](#cite_note-historymain-1)

From 2002, the European Challenge Cup winner now automatically qualified for the Heineken Cup. Toulouse's victory over French rivals [Perpignan](/wiki/USA_Perpignan "USA Perpignan") in 2003 meant that they joined Leicester as the only teams to win the title twice.[[1]](#cite_note-historymain-1) Toulouse saw a 19-point half-time lead whittled away as the Catalans staged a dramatic comeback in a match in which the strong wind and showers played a major role, but Toulouse survived to win.

In 2003–04 the [Welsh Rugby Union](/wiki/Welsh_Rugby_Union "Welsh Rugby Union") (WRU) voted to [create regions](/wiki/Introduction_of_regional_rugby_union_teams_in_Wales "Introduction of regional rugby union teams in Wales") to play in the Celtic League and represent Wales in European competition. Henceforth, Wales entered regional sides rather than the club sides that had previously competed. English side [London Wasps](/wiki/Wasps_RFC "Wasps RFC") had earned their first final appearance by beating Munster 37–32 in a Dublin semi-final while Toulouse triumphed 19–11 in an all-French contest with [Biarritz](/wiki/Biarritz_Olympique "Biarritz Olympique") in a packed Stade Chaban-Delmas in Bordeaux. The 2004 final saw Wasps defeat defending champions Toulouse 27–20 at [Twickenham](/wiki/Twickenham_Stadium "Twickenham Stadium") to win the Heineken Cup for the first time. The match was widely hailed as one of the best finals. With extra time looming at 20–20, a late opportunist try by scrum half Rob Howley settled the contest.

#### 2005–2014

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Rugby_Champions_Cup&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2005–2014")]

The tenth Heineken Cup final saw the inaugural champions Toulouse battle with rising stars Stade Français when [Murrayfield](/wiki/Murrayfield_Stadium "Murrayfield Stadium") was the first Scottish venue to host the final.[[7]](#cite_note-toulouseparis-7) [Fabien Galthié](/wiki/Fabien_Galthi%C3%A9 "Fabien Galthié")'s Paris side led until two minutes from the end of normal time before [Frédéric Michalak](/wiki/Fr%C3%A9d%C3%A9ric_Michalak "Frédéric Michalak") levelled the contest for Toulouse with his first penalty strike. He repeated this in the initial stages of extra time and then sealed his side's success with a superb opportunist drop-goal. Toulouse became the first team to win three Heineken Cup titles.[[7]](#cite_note-toulouseparis-7)

In 2006, [Munster](/wiki/Munster_Rugby "Munster Rugby") defeated Biarritz in the [Millennium Stadium](/wiki/Millennium_Stadium "Millennium Stadium"), [Cardiff](/wiki/Cardiff "Cardiff"), 23–19.[[8]](#cite_note-munster-8) It was third time lucky for the Irish provincial side, who had previously been denied the ultimate prize twice by Northampton and [Leicester](/wiki/Leicester_Tigers "Leicester Tigers") in finals, in addition to a series of tight semi-final losses. Munster's history of heartbreaking near misses, large away followings and their enduring close connection to the tournament provided much of the romantic narrative of the early years of the competition.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Wasps_HEC_Final_2007.JPG/330px-Wasps_HEC_Final_2007.JPG)](/wiki/File:Wasps_HEC_Final_2007.JPG)

[London Wasps](/wiki/Wasps_RFC "Wasps RFC") celebrate after winning the [2006–07 Heineken Cup](/wiki/2006%E2%80%9307_Heineken_Cup "2006–07 Heineken Cup").

The 2006–07 Heineken Cup would be distributed to over 100 countries following Pitch International's securing of the rights.[[10]](#cite_note-tv-10) That season was the first time in the history of the competition that two teams went unbeaten in pool play, with both [Llanelli Scarlets](/wiki/Scarlets "Scarlets") and Biarritz doing so. Biarritz went into their final match at Northampton Saints with a chance to become the first team ever to score bonus-point wins in all their pool matches, but were only able to score two of the four tries needed. Leicester defeated Llanelli Scarlets to move into the final at Twickenham, with the possibility of winning a Treble of championships on the cards, having already won the Anglo-Welsh Cup and the English Premiership. However, Wasps won the final 25 points to 9 in front of a tournament record 81,076 fans.[[11]](#cite_note-wasps-11)

During competition there was uncertainty over the future of the tournament after the 2006–07 season as French clubs had announced that they would not take part because of fixture congestion following the [Rugby World Cup](/wiki/Rugby_World_Cup "Rugby World Cup") and an ongoing dispute between English clubs and the RFU.[[12]](#cite_note-fut-12)[[13]](#cite_note-futtwo-13) It was speculated that league two teams might compete the next season, the RFU saying "If this situation is not resolved, the RFU owes it to the sport to keep this competition going...We have spoken to our FDR clubs, and if they want to compete we will support them.".[[14]](#cite_note-eng-14) A subsequent meeting led to the announcement that the tournament would be played in 2007–08, with clubs from all the six nations. On 20 May it was announced that both French and English top-tier teams would be competing[[15]](#cite_note-Competing-15)

In the 2008 final, Munster won the cup for their second time ever by beating [Toulouse](/wiki/Stade_Toulousain "Stade Toulousain") at the Millennium Stadium in Cardiff.

[Leinster](/wiki/Leinster_Rugby "Leinster Rugby") won the title in [2009](/wiki/2009_Heineken_Cup_Final "2009 Heineken Cup Final") in their first ever final after beating Munster in the semi-final in front of a then world record Rugby Union club match attendance in [Croke Park](/wiki/Croke_Park "Croke Park"). They beat the Leicester Tigers in the final at [Murrayfield Stadium](/wiki/Murrayfield_Stadium "Murrayfield Stadium") in Edinburgh. They also beat [Harlequins](/wiki/Harlequin_F.C. "Harlequin F.C.") 6–5 in the quarter-finals at [Twickenham Stoop](/wiki/Twickenham_Stoop "Twickenham Stoop"), in the famous [Bloodgate](/wiki/Bloodgate "Bloodgate") scandal.

In the 2010 final, [Toulouse](/wiki/Stade_Toulousain "Stade Toulousain") defeated [Biarritz Olympique](/wiki/Biarritz_Olympique "Biarritz Olympique") in the [Stade de France](/wiki/Stade_de_France "Stade de France") to claim their fourth title, a Heineken Cup record.

The 16th Heineken Cup tournament in 2011 resulted in an Irish province lifting the title for the fourth time in six years as [Leinster](/wiki/Leinster_Rugby "Leinster Rugby") recorded their second triumph in the competition. They defeated former multiple Heineken Cup winners [Leicester](/wiki/Leicester_Tigers "Leicester Tigers") and Toulouse in the quarter- and semi-finals. At the Millennium Stadium in Cardiff, in front of 72,000 spectators,[[16]](#cite_note-Leinster_turn_the_tide-16) [Leinster](/wiki/Leinster_Rugby "Leinster Rugby") fought back from a 22–6 half-time deficit in the final against [Northampton Saints](/wiki/Northampton_Saints "Northampton Saints"), scoring 27 unanswered points in 26 second-half minutes, winning 33–22 in one of the tournament's greatest comebacks. [Johnny Sexton](/wiki/Johnny_Sexton "Johnny Sexton") won the man-of-the-match award, having scored 28 of Leinster's points total, which included two [tries](/wiki/Try_(rugby) "Try (rugby)"), three [conversions](/wiki/Try_(rugby) "Try (rugby)"), and four [penalties](/wiki/Penalty_(rugby) "Penalty (rugby)").

Leinster successfully defended their crown in 2012 at Twickenham, eclipsing fellow Irish province and former champions Ulster 42–14 to establish the highest Heineken Cup final winning margin. The performance broke a number of Heineken Cup Final records.[[17]](#cite_note-Leinster_enter_record_books-17) Leinster became only the second team to win back-to-back titles, and the only team ever to win three championships in four years. In addition, the game had the highest attendance at a final (81,774), the highest number of tries (5) and points (42) scored by one team and the highest points difference (28).

The [final edition](/wiki/2013%E2%80%9314_Heineken_Cup "2013–14 Heineken Cup") of the tournament as constituted as the Heineken Cup was won for a second time by [Toulon](/wiki/RC_Toulonnais "RC Toulonnais") at the Millennium Stadium in Cardiff in May 2014.

### Champions Cup

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Rugby_Champions_Cup&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Champions Cup")]

#### 2014–18

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Rugby_Champions_Cup&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2014–18")]

The tournament began on 17 October 2014, with Harlequins playing Castres Olympique in the first ever Champions Cup game. Toulon retained their title, beating [Clermont](/wiki/ASM_Clermont_Auvergne "ASM Clermont Auvergne") 24–18 in a repeat of the [2013 Heineken Cup Final](/wiki/2013_Heineken_Cup_Final "2013 Heineken Cup Final"), thereby becoming the first club to win three European titles in a row.[[18]](#cite_note-bbc.co.uk-18)

Following the [November 2015 Paris attacks](/wiki/November_2015_Paris_attacks "November 2015 Paris attacks"), all Round 1 games due to take place in France that weekend were called off, along with the Round 2 fixture between [Stade Français](/wiki/Stade_Fran%C3%A7ais "Stade Français") and [Munster](/wiki/Munster_Rugby "Munster Rugby").[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20) Rescheduling of some matches was difficult, partly caused by fixture congestion due to the [2015 Rugby World Cup](/wiki/2015_Rugby_World_Cup "2015 Rugby World Cup").[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)

[Saracens](/wiki/Saracens_F.C. "Saracens F.C.") won their first title defeating Racing 92 in Lyon 21–9 in 2016 final and followed it up with their second in 2017, beating [Clermont](/wiki/ASM_Clermont_Auvergne "ASM Clermont Auvergne") 28–17 in Edinburgh.

In 2017–18 season, [Leinster](/wiki/Leinster_Rugby "Leinster Rugby") overcame the "pool of death" consisting of Glasgow Warriors (who finished the 2017–18 season top of the Pro14), Montpellier (who finished the 2017–18 season top of the TOP 14) and Exeter (who finished the 2017–18 season top of the English Premiership), beating all three teams both home and away. Leinster went on to face the back to back Champions [Saracens](/wiki/Saracens_RFC "Saracens RFC"), dispatching a defeat at the Aviva Stadium in Dublin, to set up a semi-final against reigning Pro12 champions Scarlets.[[24]](#cite_note-24) Leinster defeated Scarlets to face Racing 92 in Champions Cup Final in Bilbao. [Leinster](/wiki/Leinster_Rugby "Leinster Rugby") defeated Racing 92 by a scoreline of 15–12, becoming only the second team in history to earn four European titles.[[25]](#cite_note-25) [Leinster](/wiki/Leinster_Rugby "Leinster Rugby") also won the Pro14 title to become the first Pro14 side to win the domestic-European 'Double'.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

### Heineken Champions Cup

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Rugby_Champions_Cup&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Heineken Champions Cup")]

#### 2018–2020

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Rugby_Champions_Cup&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 2018–2020")]

[Saracens](/wiki/Saracens_F.C. "Saracens F.C.") won the 2018–19 competition, defeating defending champions [Leinster](/wiki/Leinster_Rugby "Leinster Rugby") 20–10 in the final.[[27]](#cite_note-27) Saracens were in breach of the Premiership salary cap during this edition and the previous year, in which they qualified for the 2018–19 cup.[[28]](#cite_note-ruck190511-28)[[29]](#cite_note-huge20191106-29) However, the EPCR have confirmed that Premiership ruling will not affect the results of the Heineken Champions Cup for 2018–19 or previous years, stating: "The Saracens decision is based on Gallagher Premiership Rugby regulations and does not affect the club's European record or current status in the Heineken Champions Cup."[[30]](#cite_note-pundit191105-30) There were no Saracens representatives at the launch of the 2019–20 competition, held in Cardiff on 6 November 2019. EPCR released a statement saying they were "disappointed to learn of Saracens' decision to make their club representatives unavailable for today's official 2019–20 season launch".[[31]](#cite_note-31)

#### 2020–2023

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Rugby_Champions_Cup&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 2020–2023")]

Because of the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic") the [2020–21 competition](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_European_Rugby_Champions_Cup "2020–21 European Rugby Champions Cup") took on a revised format based on a hybrid of the [round-robin](/wiki/Round-robin_tournament "Round-robin tournament") and [Swiss systems](/wiki/Swiss-system_tournament "Swiss-system tournament").[[32]](#cite_note-:0-32) A similar, but revised 24 team format took place for [2021–22](/wiki/2021%E2%80%9322_European_Rugby_Champions_Cup "2021–22 European Rugby Champions Cup") and [2022-23](/wiki/2022%E2%80%9323_European_Rugby_Champions_Cup "2022–23 European Rugby Champions Cup").[[33]](#cite_note-33)

### Investec Champions Cup

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Rugby_Champions_Cup&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Investec Champions Cup")]

On 31 August 2023, [Investec](/wiki/Investec "Investec"), a bank and wealth management company with operations in South Africa and Europe, and the European Professional Club Rugby announced Investec would take over as title sponsor of the competition.[[34]](#cite_note-34) The tournament moved away from the COVID-19 influenced hybrid system back toward a more straightforward, if still modified, round-robin pool format, while retaining the balance of four pool matches and four knock-out weekends.

## Format

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Rugby_Champions_Cup&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Format")]

### Qualification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Rugby_Champions_Cup&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Qualification")]

Typically, a total of 24 teams qualify for the competition, the same number as used to qualify for the Heineken Cup. At least 23 of the 24 teams qualify automatically based on position in their respective leagues. The winner of the Challenge Cup will earn a place regardless of league position.

Team distribution is typically:

* England: 8 teams, based on position in [Premiership Rugby](/wiki/Premiership_Rugby "Premiership Rugby") allowing for the Challenge Cup entry route;
* France: 8 teams, based on position in the [Top 14](/wiki/Top_14 "Top 14"), again allowing for the Challenge Cup entry route;
* South Africa, Ireland, Italy, Scotland and Wales: 8 teams, based on performance in the [United Rugby Championship](/wiki/United_Rugby_Championship "United Rugby Championship") (previously the Pro14).
  + From 2014 to 2017, the best placed team from each country in the Pro14 qualified for the competition, along with the best three remaining teams regardless of nationality;
  + In 2017, it was announced that this format would change.[[35]](#cite_note-qualy_changes-35) Starting with qualification for the 2018–19 competition, the Pro14 places would be assigned regardless of nationality, rather than the requirement that at least one team qualify from each participating nation.

#### 20th team qualification (-2020)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Rugby_Champions_Cup&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: 20th team qualification (-2020)")]

Until 2018–19, the final team each season qualified through a play-off competition between the best placed unqualified teams.

* For the 2014–15 season, this was a two legged play-off between the seventh-placed teams in the Top 14 and the English Premiership. The team with the highest aggregate score over the two legs advancing to the Champions Cup.
* For the 2015–16 season, there was a three-team play-off; the seventh-placed team in the English Premiership, or the winners of the 2014–15 European Rugby Challenge Cup if members of the English Premiership and not already qualified, would play the eighth-placed (or highest non-qualified) team from the Pro14, with the winner playing the seventh-placed team in the Top 14.
* To facilitate Rugby World Cup 2015, there were no play-offs for the 2016–17 Champions Cup with the 20th place going to the winner of the 2016 Challenge Cup if not already qualified.
* For 2017–18, the play-off format included four clubs with a second Pro14 club competing. If not already qualified, the winner of the Challenge Cup will take the place in the play-offs of the seventh-ranked club in the English Premiership and Top 14, and will also take the place of the second Pro14 club if applicable.[[36]](#cite_note-qualification-36)
* In May 2017, it was announced that, starting with qualification for the 2018–19 Champions Cup, the play-off will be scrapped in favour of awarding the final berth using the following criteria:[[35]](#cite_note-qualy_changes-35)[[36]](#cite_note-qualification-36)

1. Champions Cup winner, if not already qualified.
2. [European Rugby Challenge Cup](/wiki/European_Rugby_Challenge_Cup "European Rugby Challenge Cup") winner, if not already qualified.
3. Challenge Cup losing finalist, if not already qualified.
4. Challenge Cup semi-finalist, if one has not already qualified (or the winner of a play-off between the semi-finalists, if both have not already qualified).
5. Highest ranked non-qualified club by virtue of league position from the same league as the Champions Cup winner.

### Competition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Rugby_Champions_Cup&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Competition")]

#### Group stage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Rugby_Champions_Cup&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Group stage")]

For the pool stage teams are placed into pools via a draw. The teams are ranked based on domestic league performance the previous season, and arranged into four tiers. Teams are then drawn from the tiers into pools at random, with the restriction that no team will be drawn in the same pool as another team from the same league and tier.[[37]](#cite_note-pool_draw_exp-37) The nature of the draw means that sides will have a relatively even spread of matches across venues, leagues and tiers.

Teams will play the two other teams in their pool from each different league once, at home or away, and match points will be awarded depending on the result of each game, with teams receiving four points for a win, and two for a draw. Teams can also earn bonus points for scoring four or more tries and/or for losing a match by seven points or fewer.[[38]](#cite_note-rules-38)

Following the completion of the pool stage, 16 teams qualify for the knock-out stage of the Champions Cup with a further 4 of the remaining 8 dropping into the Challenge Cup. The final four teams are eliminated[[39]](#cite_note-RFU_release-39)

#### Knock-out stage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Rugby_Champions_Cup&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Knock-out stage")]

The sixteen remaining teams are seeded from 1–16 based on performance in their respective pool. The top two teams in each of the four pools gain home advantage in the round of 16, with the teams finishing third and fourth playing away The quarter-finals are unbracketed, and follow the standard 1v8, 2v7, 3v6, 4v5 format, as found in the Heineken Cup.[[38]](#cite_note-rules-38)[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]

The winners of the quarter-finals will contest the two semi-finals, Up to and including the 2014–15 season, matches and home country advantage were determined by a draw by EPCR.

In 2015–16, EPCR decided to put a new procedure in place. In lieu of the draw that used to determine the semi-final pairing, EPCR announced that the fixed semi-final bracket would be set in advance, and that the home team would be designated based on *"performances by clubs during the pool stages as well as the achievement of a winning a quarter-final match away from home"*. Semi-final matches must have been played at a neutral ground in the designated home team's country.

Since 2018–19, the higher-seeded team will have home country/venue advantage for each semi-final regardless of whether they won their quarter-final at home or on the road.[[40]](#cite_note-40) The EPCR may now also use its discretion to allow semi-finals to be played at a qualified club's home venue.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

The winners of the semi-finals will contest the final, which will be held in May each season.[[42]](#cite_note-EPCR_statement-42)

#### 2020–3

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Rugby_Champions_Cup&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: 2020–3")]

Because of the [COVID-19 pandemic in Europe](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Europe "COVID-19 pandemic in Europe") the [2020–21 competition](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_European_Rugby_Champions_Cup "2020–21 European Rugby Champions Cup") took on a revised format. In this edition, the teams were split up into two separate pools of 12, in which they would play four games against opponents from their pool, before the top four teams from each of the two pools advanced to the knockout stage, made up of two-legged quarterfinals, and a single legged semi-finals and final. However, due to the increasing spread of the virus, only two rounds were played before the competition was suspended, and instead the top eight teams from each of the two pools advanced to the knockout stage, with all matches being single-legged affairs.

A similar format remained for [2021–22](/wiki/2021%E2%80%9322_European_Rugby_Champions_Cup "2021–22 European Rugby Champions Cup"), with the top eight teams from each of the two pools advancing to the knockout stage, which featured a two-legged Round of 16 before a single-legged quarterfinals, semi-finals and final. The [2022-23](/wiki/2022-23_European_Rugby_Champions_Cup "2022-23 European Rugby Champions Cup") campaign will retain the same pool format, but, like 2020–21, all knockout stage games will be single-legged.

#### 2023–present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Rugby_Champions_Cup&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: 2023–present")]

A further change took place from 2023-4, with four pools of six teams. The winners of the three leagues (the URC, the Premiership, and the Top 14), along with the winner of the previous season's Champions Cup, are drawn into separate pools. Where the Champions Cup winner also wins its own league, the Champions Cup runner-up takes the fourth place in the draw. The other teams are drawn so that each six-team pool contains two teams from each of the three leagues.

Within the four pools, each of the teams plays once against the other four teams that are not from its own league, with two games at home and two away. The top four teams from each pool qualify for the round of 16, with the top two teams enjoying home advantage. The fifth-placed team in each pool drops into the round of 16 of the Challenge Cup. The bottom-placed team is eliminated.

The four knock-out rounds continue to be single-legged. In the round of 16 and quarter-finals, the team with a higher ranking from the pool stages has home stadium advantage. The semi-finals are held in a venue selected by EPCR in the country of the higher-ranked team. The final is in a predetermined major stadium.

## Finals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Rugby_Champions_Cup&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Finals")]

See also: [List of European Rugby Champions Cup finals](/wiki/List_of_European_Rugby_Champions_Cup_finals "List of European Rugby Champions Cup finals")

[Twickenham](/wiki/Twickenham_Stadium "Twickenham Stadium") has been the most common venue for the final, holding the event on six occasions. The [Millennium Stadium](/wiki/Millennium_Stadium "Millennium Stadium") in Cardiff has held five finals, and its [predecessor stadium on the same site](/wiki/National_Stadium,_Cardiff "National Stadium, Cardiff") a further two, but the latter stadium was completely demolished to build the Millennium, and the two are treated as two separate venues. The site, however, holds the record for most finals, seven. The final has been held once in a nation that is not part of the competition; [San Mamés Stadium](/wiki/San_Mam%C3%A9s_Stadium_(2013) "San Mamés Stadium (2013)"), [Bilbao](/wiki/Bilbao "Bilbao") in Spain held the 2018 final. As of 2024 the final has never been held in South Africa or Italy. [Nigel Owens](/wiki/Nigel_Owens "Nigel Owens") from Wales holds the record for most appearances by a referee in the final, with seven.

#### European Player of the Year

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Rugby_Champions_Cup&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: European Player of the Year")]

The European Player of the Year award was introduced by ERC in 2010. Ronan O'Gara received the inaugural award, being recognised as the best player over the first 15 years of ERC tournaments.[[65]](#cite_note-68) Following the creation of the European Rugby Champions Cup, the new organisers, EPCR, continued to award a Player of the Year accolade, with the first going to [Clermont](/wiki/ASM_Clermont_Auvergne "ASM Clermont Auvergne") full-back [Nick Abendanon](/wiki/Nick_Abendanon "Nick Abendanon"). In the 2016/17 season it was announced that the award would change name to the Anthony Foley Memorial Award after the passing of [Munster](/wiki/Munster_Rugby "Munster Rugby") Head Coach. The first winner of this since the change in title was [Saracens](/wiki/Saracens_F.C. "Saracens F.C.") fly half [Owen Farrell](/wiki/Owen_Farrell "Owen Farrell").[[66]](#cite_note-69)

## Sponsorship and suppliers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Rugby_Champions_Cup&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Sponsorship and suppliers")]

### Sponsors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Rugby_Champions_Cup&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Sponsors")]

During the creation of the Champions Cup, former organisers ERC had been criticised for "failing to maximise the commercial potential" of the Heineken Cup. New organisers EPCR pledged to move from a single title sponsor format to a [Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League")-style partner system, with 2–3 primary partners projected for the inaugural tournament and 5 being the ultimate target. However, only [Heineken](/wiki/Heineken "Heineken") agreed to sign up for the 2014–15 season, at a much reduced price from that which they had been paying previously.[[83]](#cite_note-irishtimes.com-86)[[84]](#cite_note-walesonline.co.uk-87)

#### Principal partners

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Rugby_Champions_Cup&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Principal partners")]

* [Heineken](/wiki/Heineken_International "Heineken International") (1995–2014; 2018–)

:   :   Heineken, who had sponsored the Heineken Cup since 1995, signed on as the first partner for the Champions Cup in 2014, and were credited as the *Founding Partner of European Rugby*. They returned to the competition as title sponsors in 2018, resulting in it being renamed as the "Heineken Champions Cup".[[85]](#cite_note-88) Due to French restrictions on alcohol advertising, it is known as the "H Cup" in France.[[86]](#cite_note-89)

* [Turkish Airlines](/wiki/Turkish_Airlines "Turkish Airlines") (2015–2017)

:   :   Announced as the second principal partner at the 2015–16 tournament launch, signing on for three seasons[[87]](#cite_note-90)

## Trophy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Rugby_Champions_Cup&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Trophy")]

The European Rugby Champions Cup trophy was unveiled in October 2014.[[88]](#cite_note-91)

Crafted by [Thomas Lyte](/wiki/Thomas_Lyte "Thomas Lyte"),[[89]](#cite_note-92) the trophy is made of mixed metals including sterling silver and 18ct gold plating. The cup is designed around the idea of the star representing European rugby, including the previous 19 seasons of European rugby, as the Heineken Cup.

The 13.5 kg, five-handled trophy, creates a star shape when viewed from the top, while when viewed from the side, the top of the trophy has a coronet effect, which designers said was to reflect the crowning of the Kings of Europe. The base of the trophy contains the crests of the 10 clubs that won the Heineken Cup, to further reinforce the link between the old and new European competitions[[90]](#cite_note-93)

## Attendance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Rugby_Champions_Cup&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Attendance")]

This lists the average attendances for each season's European Cup competition, as well as the total attendance and highest attendance for that season. The final is typically the most-attended match, as it is generally held in a larger stadium than any club's home venue.

The highest attended match of the 2002–03 competition was a quarter-final between [Leinster](/wiki/Leinster_Rugby "Leinster Rugby") and [Biarritz](/wiki/Biarritz_Olympique "Biarritz Olympique") before 46,000 fans at [Lansdowne Road](/wiki/Lansdowne_Road "Lansdowne Road") in Dublin.

The 2009 final held at [Murrayfield Stadium](/wiki/Murrayfield_Stadium "Murrayfield Stadium") in Edinburgh was only the third most-attended match that season. The most-attended match was a semi-final between Irish rivals [Leinster](/wiki/Leinster_Rugby "Leinster Rugby") and [Munster](/wiki/Munster_Rugby "Munster Rugby") played in [Croke Park](/wiki/Croke_Park "Croke Park") in Dublin. The attendance of 82,208 set what was then a world record for a club match in the sport's history.[[96]](#cite_note-99) Second on that season's list was a pool match between [Stade Français](/wiki/Stade_Fran%C3%A7ais "Stade Français") and [Harlequins](/wiki/Harlequin_F.C. "Harlequin F.C.") that drew 76,569 to [Stade de France](/wiki/Stade_de_France "Stade de France") in Paris (a venue that Stade Français has used for select home matches since [2005](/wiki/2005%E2%80%9306_Top_14_season "2005–06 Top 14 season")).

While the 2010–11 tournament's highest attended match was unsurprisingly the final, the second-highest attended match was notable in that it was held in Spain. [Perpignan hosted Toulon](/wiki/2010%E2%80%9311_Heineken_Cup#Knock-out_stage "2010–11 Heineken Cup") in a quarter-final before a sellout crowd of 55,000 at the [Olympic Stadium](/wiki/Estadi_Ol%C3%ADmpic_Llu%C3%ADs_Companys "Estadi Olímpic Lluís Companys") in Barcelona, Spain.

## Controversies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Rugby_Champions_Cup&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Controversies")]

### Disagreements over structure & governance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Rugby_Champions_Cup&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Disagreements over structure & governance")]

English and French rugby union clubs had long held concerns over the format and structure of the Heineken Cup organised by [European Rugby Cup](/wiki/European_Rugby_Cup "European Rugby Cup") (ERC), predominantly in relation to the distribution of funds and an imbalance in the qualification process.[[98]](#cite_note-101) Some proposals had been made that, in future, rather than Ireland, Wales, Scotland and Italy each sending their top-placed teams in the Pro14 to the Heineken Cup, the top teams from the league as a whole should be sent, regardless of nationality. This founding principle was eventually conceded however, when it was agreed that the top-placed teams from the four should participate in the new European competition.[[99]](#cite_note-102)

In June 2012, following that year's final, Premiership Rugby and the [Ligue Nationale de Rugby](/wiki/National_Rugby_League_(France) "National Rugby League (France)") (LNR), on behalf of the English and French clubs respectively, gave ERC two years' notice of withdrawing from the Heineken Cup and also the second-tier Challenge Cup competitions from the start of the 2014–15 season.[[100]](#cite_note-103) Soon after, in September, Premiership Rugby announced a new four-year TV deal worth £152 million with [BT Sport](/wiki/BT_Sport "BT Sport") including rights for English clubs' European games - which had previously been the sole responsibility of ERC. ERC responded with claims that Premiership Rugby did not have the rights to a European tournament and announced a four-year deal with [Sky Sports](/wiki/Sky_Sports "Sky Sports"). The actions of Premiership Rugby were said to have "thrown northern hemisphere rugby into disarray".[[101]](#cite_note-104)

Subsequently, in September 2013, the English and French clubs announced their intention to organise their own tournament, to be named the Rugby Champions Cup, from 2014 to 2015 season onwards, and invited other European clubs, provinces, and regions to join them. The [IRB](/wiki/World_Rugby "World Rugby") (now World Rugby) stepped into the debate at the same time to announce its opposition to the creation of a breakaway tournament.[[102]](#cite_note-105) In October 2013, Regional Rugby Wales, on behalf of the four Welsh regions, confirmed its full support for the proposed new Rugby Champions Cup.[[103]](#cite_note-106) Negotiations for both a new Heineken Cup and Rugby Champions Cup were then ongoing.[[104]](#cite_note-107)

On 10 April 2014, following almost two years of negotiations, a statement was released under the aegis of [European Professional Club Rugby](/wiki/European_Professional_Club_Rugby "European Professional Club Rugby") (EPCR) announcing that the nine stakeholders to the new competition, the six unions, and three umbrella club organisations (Premiership Rugby, LNR, and Regional Rugby Wales), had signed Heads of Agreement for the formation of the European Rugby Champions Cup, the [European Rugby Challenge Cup](/wiki/European_Rugby_Challenge_Cup "European Rugby Challenge Cup") and a new, third tournament, initially called the Qualifying Competition and now known as the [European Rugby Continental Shield](/wiki/European_Rugby_Continental_Shield "European Rugby Continental Shield").[[105]](#cite_note-108)[[106]](#cite_note-109) On the same day, BT and Sky announced an agreement that divided coverage of the new European competitions. Both will split the pool matches, quarter-finals, and semi-finals equally, and both will broadcast the final. BT will get first choice of English Premiership club matches in the Champions Cup, with Sky receiving the same privilege for the Challenge Cup.[[107]](#cite_note-110)

Premiership Rugby and LNR were described as having employed "bully-boy tactics" by *[The Irish Times](/wiki/The_Irish_Times "The Irish Times")*.[[83]](#cite_note-irishtimes.com-86)

### Organisation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Rugby_Champions_Cup&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Organisation")]

Shortly after the establishment of [European Professional Club Rugby](/wiki/European_Professional_Club_Rugby "European Professional Club Rugby") (EPCR) to administer the new competition from a new base in Neuchatel, Switzerland, the running of the inaugural 2014–15 tournament was subcontracted to the organisation it had been meant to replace, Dublin-based [European Rugby Cup](/wiki/European_Rugby_Cup "European Rugby Cup") (ERC). This was despite the latter having been described by chairman of [Premiership Rugby](/wiki/Premiership_Rugby "Premiership Rugby"), Quentin Smith, as "no longer fit for purpose". This was described as "something of an about-turn" by *[The Daily Telegraph](/wiki/The_Daily_Telegraph "The Daily Telegraph")*.[[108]](#cite_note-111)

EPCR were still looking to hire a permanent chairman and director-general more than a year after their establishment.[[84]](#cite_note-walesonline.co.uk-87)

### 2015 final

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Rugby_Champions_Cup&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: 2015 final")]

The inaugural [Champions Cup final](/wiki/2015_European_Rugby_Champions_Cup_Final "2015 European Rugby Champions Cup Final") was brought forward by three weeks due to a French desire not to interrupt their [domestic playoffs](/wiki/2014%E2%80%9315_Top_14_season#Playoffs "2014–15 Top 14 season"). This was said to have "devalued" and "diminished the status of the occasion as the pinnacle of European club rugby".[[83]](#cite_note-irishtimes.com-86)[[84]](#cite_note-walesonline.co.uk-87)

While the 2015 Heineken Cup final had been due to take place at the [San Siro](/wiki/San_Siro "San Siro") in [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan"), the first European final to take place in Italy, the new organisers decided to move it to [Twickenham Stadium](/wiki/Twickenham_Stadium "Twickenham Stadium") in [London](/wiki/London "London") in order to "guarantee the best possible financial return to clubs".[[84]](#cite_note-walesonline.co.uk-87) However, with less than two weeks to go before the final took place, it was reported that fewer than half of the stadium's 82,000 seats had been sold, with just 8,000 French supporters travelling to London to watch [Toulon](/wiki/RC_Toulonnais "RC Toulonnais") face [Clermont](/wiki/ASM_Clermont_Auvergne "ASM Clermont Auvergne").[[109]](#cite_note-112) The organisers subsequently made "free" tickets available on Ticketmaster (with only a £2 booking fee applicable), before admitting to this being a mistake – the offer supposed to have been linked to a purchase of a Premiership final ticket. This was described as an "embarrassing fiasco" by the *[Western Mail](/wiki/Western_Mail_(Wales) "Western Mail (Wales)")* in Wales.[[84]](#cite_note-walesonline.co.uk-87)[[110]](#cite_note-113) 56,622 fans subsequently attended the game. EPCR were said to have "failed on many levels" by *[The Irish Times](/wiki/The_Irish_Times "The Irish Times")*, with the attendance figure for the final "a fitting postscript to the hastily-convened decider to what was, after all the brinkmanship, a hastily-convened tournament".[[83]](#cite_note-irishtimes.com-86)

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Rugby_Champions_Cup&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-43)** [Chris White](/wiki/Chris_White_(rugby_union) "Chris White (rugby union)") started the [2001 Heineken Cup final](/wiki/2001_Heineken_Cup_final "2001 Heineken Cup final") as referee, before withdrawing in the 12th minute due to injury, to be replaced by reserve official [Tony Spreadbury](/wiki/Tony_Spreadbury "Tony Spreadbury") .
2. **[^](#cite_ref-45)** The 2020 final was played behind closed doors due to the [COVID-19 pandemic in Europe](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Europe "COVID-19 pandemic in Europe").[[43]](#cite_note-behind_closed_doors-44)
3. **[^](#cite_ref-46)** Capacity limited to 10,000 due to [national restrictions](/wiki/The_Health_Protection_(Coronavirus,_Restrictions)_(Steps)_(England)_Regulations_2021 "The Health Protection (Coronavirus, Restrictions) (Steps) (England) Regulations 2021") surrounding the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic").
